claim: 75% of lost cats were found within 500m of their escape point, establishing a tight displacement radius as the primary spatial prior for a displaced indoor animal.
stance_target: short-radius prior for displaced/frightened animals; analogous prior for xenophobic lost dogs
scope_conditions: Huang et al. 2018 Animals; n=1,210 cats globally; self-selected retrospective online survey; applies to all cat categories combined; cats not dogs
quoted_support: 75% of cats were found within 500 m of the point of escape.
numbers: 75% of cats within 500m of escape point

claim: Outdoor-access cats traveled significantly farther than indoor-only cats when lost: 75th-percentile distance 1,609m vs. 137m (p≤0.001), demonstrating that prior habitat and behavioral freedom sharply bifurcate expected displacement radius.
stance_target: prior behavioral freedom (outdoor vs. indoor) as a key predictor of lost animal travel distance
scope_conditions: Huang 2018; n=1,210 cats; global self-selected sample; translatable to lost-dog prior calibration by activity/roaming history
quoted_support: Up to 75% of cats with outdoor access traveled 1609 m, further than the distance traveled by indoor-only cats (137 m; p ≤ 0.001).

claim: Recovery probability for missing cats decays sharply after 7 days: 34% recovered alive within 7 days; very few found alive after 90 days — establishing a temporal phase transition around day 7.
stance_target: temporal decay in recovery probability and the critical early-phase window
scope_conditions: Huang 2018; n=1,210 cats; global retrospective online survey
quoted_support: Only 61% were found within one year, with 34% recovered alive by the owner within 7 days. Few cats were found alive after 90 days.

claim: Physical search is the most effective evidence-supported recovery method for missing cats; a physical search within the first week significantly increases probability of finding the cat alive.
stance_target: early physical ground search as highest-value intervention in lost pet recovery
scope_conditions: Huang 2018; effect size borderline (p=0.073); global self-selected sample
quoted_support: There was evidence that physical searching increased the chance of finding the cat alive (p = 0.073)... These findings suggest that thorough physical searching is a useful strategy, and should be conducted within the first week after cats go missing.
numbers: p=0.073 for physical search effect on alive recovery

claim: Cats considered 'highly curious' by personality were significantly more likely to be found inside someone else's home, demonstrating that personality/temperament type predicts specific recovery location category.
stance_target: personality/temperament as a predictor of spatial settling location for lost animals
scope_conditions: Huang 2018; subjective owner-reported personality; global sample; cats only
quoted_support: Cats considered to be highly curious in nature were more likely to be found inside someone else's house compared to other personality types.
